questions.questionText: Hyperplasia is defined as an increase in the:
questions.optionA: Conversion of one adult cell type to another
questions.optionC: Number of cells in an organ or tissue
questions.explanation: Hyperplasia refers to an increased number of cells (e.g., hormonal hyperplasia in pregnancy).

questions.questionText: Barrett's esophagus is an example of which type of cellular adaptation?
questions.explanation: Squamous epithelium of the esophagus changes to columnar epithelium due to chronic acid reflux.

questions.questionText: Coagulative necrosis is classically seen in all ischemic infarctions EXCEPT in the:
questions.explanation: Ischemic injury in the brain leads to liquefactive necrosis, not coagulative.

questions.questionText: Caseous necrosis is most characteristic of which infectious disease?
questions.explanation: Caseous ('cheese-like') necrosis is the hallmark of tuberculous granulomas.

questions.questionText: The earliest ultrastructural change visible in reversible cell injury is:
questions.explanation: Mitochondrial swelling and ER dilation (cellular swelling) occur early due to ATP depletion.

questions.questionText: Which of the following describes nuclear fragmentation into small dust-like particles during cell death?
questions.explanation: Karyorrhexis is the destructive fragmentation of the nucleus of a dying cell.

questions.questionText: Fat necrosis is most commonly associated with severe inflammatory disease of the:
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipases release fatty acids which combine with calcium (saponification).

questions.questionText: Lipofuscin pigment is best described as a sign of:
questions.optionA: Free radical injury and 'wear and tear'
questions.explanation: Lipofuscin represents complexes of lipid and protein from free-radical catalyzed peroxidation.

questions.questionText: Dystrophic calcification occurs primarily in:
questions.optionC: Dead or degenerating tissues with normal serum calcium
questions.explanation: Dystrophic calcification occurs locally in abnormal tissue, independently of absolute calcium levels.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is an anti-apoptotic gene?
questions.explanation: Bcl-2 is the prototype anti-apoptotic protein that prevents cytochrome c release.

questions.questionText: The first vascular response to an acute injury is:
questions.explanation: Brief neurogenic vasoconstriction is immediately followed by characteristic vasodilation.

questions.questionText: The predominant inflammatory cell in the first 6-24 hours of acute inflammation is the:
questions.explanation: Neutrophils are the first responders, later replaced by monocytes/macrophages.

questions.questionText: Which mediator is primarily responsible for the pain of acute inflammation?
questions.explanation: Bradykinin and PGE2 sensitive nerve endings to cause pain.

questions.questionText: Opsonization of bacteria to enhance phagocytosis is primarily mediated by:
questions.explanation: C3b and IgG act as powerful opsonins.

questions.questionText: Which selectin is expressed on endothelial cells and mediates early leukocyte rolling?
questions.explanation: Endothelial (E) and Platelet (P) selectins mediate leukocyte rolling.

questions.questionText: Firm adhesion of leukocytes to the endothelium is mediated primarily by:
questions.explanation: Integrins (LFA-1, MAC-1) strongly bind ICAMs during firm adhesion.

questions.questionText: The hallmark histological feature of granulomatous inflammation is the presence of:
questions.explanation: Aggregates of activated macrophages resembling epithelial cells are required for the diagnosis of a granuloma.

questions.questionText: Which cytokine is crucial for maintaining granuloma formation in tuberculosis?
questions.explanation: Th1 cells secrete IFN-y to continuously activate macrophages.

questions.questionText: A scar formed from excessive accumulation of collagen that grows beyond the boundaries of the original wound is a:
questions.explanation: Unlike hypertrophic scars, keloids do not regress and expand beyond the injury site.

questions.questionText: Type I collagen is replacing Type III collagen during which phase of wound healing?
questions.explanation: Matrix metalloproteinases degrade Type III to be replaced by stronger Type I during remodeling.

questions.questionText: Anasarca is best defined as:
questions.optionA: A localized collection of blood outside blood vessels
questions.optionC: Fluid transudation in the pleural space
questions.optionD: Accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity
questions.explanation: Anasarca is generalized massive edema of subcutaneous tissues.

questions.questionText: Which of the following promotes the formation of a transudate rather than an exudate?
questions.explanation: Transudates occur due to hydrostatic or oncotic pressure imbalances without increased permeability.

questions.questionText: Heart failure cells in the lungs are:
questions.explanation: Chronic passive congestion of the lungs leads to RBC extravasation, phagocytosed by macrophages.

questions.questionText: The lines of Zahn are characteristic finding in:
questions.explanation: Alternating pale and dark layers demonstrate thrombosis occurring in flowing blood.

questions.questionText: Virchow's triad includes all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Endothelial injury, abnormal blood flow (stasis), and hypercoagulability make up the triad.

questions.questionText: The most common source of pulmonary emboli is:
questions.optionC: Deep veins of the lower extremities
questions.explanation: More than 95% of pulmonary emboli arise from DVT above the knee.

questions.questionText: Paradoxical emboli characteristically pass through the:
questions.explanation: Venous emboli can enter the systemic circulation bypassing the lungs via an ASD/PFO.

questions.questionText: A wedge-shaped, pale (white) infarct is typically seen in the:
questions.explanation: Solid organs with end-arterial circulations (kidney, heart, spleen) develop pale infarcts.

questions.questionText: Red (hemorrhagic) infarcts typically occur in tissues with:
questions.explanation: Lungs and dual-circulation organs suffer red infarcts due to bleeding into the necrotic area.

questions.questionText: The primary pathophysiological mechanism in septic shock is:
questions.optionC: Massive systemic vasodilation and increased vascular permeability
questions.explanation: Endotoxins/Cytokines cause systemic vasodilation resulting in profound hypotension.

questions.questionText: A benign tumor of glandular epithelium is called an:
questions.explanation: Adenoma denotes a benign epithelial neoplasm from glands.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most reliable morphologic criterion distinguishing a malignant tumor from a benign one?
questions.explanation: Metastasis is the absolute hallmark of malignancy.

questions.questionText: Carcinoma in situ is defined as:
questions.optionB: Invasion of tumor cells into the underlying stroma
questions.optionC: A benign tumor with a high risk of malignant transformation
questions.optionD: Dysplasia involving the full thickness of the epithelium without breaching the basement membrane
questions.explanation: CIS has all cytological features of malignancy but lacks invasion.

questions.questionText: The p53 gene product functions primarily to:
questions.optionB: Promote progression from G2 to M phase
questions.optionC: Arrest the cell cycle in G1 to allow for DNA repair
questions.explanation: p53 'guardian of the genome' halts cell cycle to repair DNA, or triggers apoptosis if unrepairable.

questions.questionText: A classical example of a chemical carcinogen causing hepatocellular carcinoma is:
questions.explanation: Aflatoxins produced by Aspergillus contaminate grains and peanuts, causing HCC.

questions.questionText: Which virus is causally associated with Burkitt lymphoma?
questions.explanation: EBV is strongly associated with endemic Burkitt lymphoma.

questions.questionText: Rb (Retinoblastoma gene) strictly regulates the cell cycle transition from:
questions.explanation: Hypophosphorylated Rb binds and inhibits E2F, blocking G1 to S progression.

questions.questionText: The grading of a cancer is based on:
questions.optionA: The degree of cellular differentiation and mitotic activity
questions.optionB: The extent of spread to lymph nodes
questions.optionC: The size of the primary tumor
questions.explanation: Grading is histological (how abnormal the cells look). Staging is clinical/pathological (extent of spread).

questions.questionText: Which tumor marker is most useful for monitoring recurrence of colon cancer?
questions.explanation: CEA is elevated in colorectal carcinoma.

questions.questionText: Tumor cells often shift their metabolism to aerobic glycolysis (Warburg effect) to:
questions.optionA: Provide metabolic intermediates for cellular biosynthesis
questions.optionC: Maximize ATP production per glucose molecule
questions.explanation: The Warburg effect allows tumor cells to use carbon from glucose for rapid biomass synthesis.

questions.questionText: Anaphylaxis is a classic example of which type of hypersensitivity reaction?
questions.explanation: IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation causes Type I hypersensitivity.

questions.questionText: Goodpasture syndrome represents which type of hypersensitivity?
questions.explanation: Antibodies directed against fixed basement membrane (anti-GBM) is Type II.

questions.questionText: Serum sickness and systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) primarily involve which hypersensitivity mechanism?
questions.explanation: Deposition of circulating immune complexes causes Type III hypersensitivity.

questions.questionText: The tuberculin (PPD) skin test reaction is mediated by:
questions.explanation: It is a classical Delayed-Type Hypersensitivity (Type IV) response.

questions.questionText: Which autoantibody is highly specific (but not sensitive) for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)?
questions.explanation: Anti-dsDNA and Anti-Smith are highly specific markers for SLE.

questions.questionText: Sjogren syndrome is characterized by the destruction of:
questions.explanation: Lymphocytic infiltration destroys exocrine glands causing dry eyes and dry mouth.

questions.questionText: The primary target of the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) is the:
questions.explanation: HIV uses the CD4 molecule as its primary receptor to infect helper T cells.

questions.questionText: A defining opportunistic infection in AIDS that presents as severe pneumonia is caused by:
questions.explanation: Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP) is a classic AIDS-defining illness.

questions.questionText: Which condition is characterized by amyloid deposition composed of AL protein?
questions.explanation: AL amyloid consists of immunoglobulin light chains produced by plasma cell dyscrasias.

questions.questionText: Hyperacute transplant rejection is mediated by:
questions.optionA: Donor T cells attacking host tissues
questions.optionB: Preformed host antibodies against donor antigens
questions.explanation: Pre-existing antibodies cause immediate thrombosis and graft necrosis.

questions.questionText: The hallmark of iron deficiency anemia in a peripheral blood smear is:
questions.explanation: Lack of iron impairs hemoglobin synthesis, producing small, pale RBCs.

questions.questionText: Megaloblastic anemia is most commonly caused by a deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Impaired DNA synthesis leads to large, immature RBC precursors (megaloblasts).

questions.questionText: The finding of Heinz bodies and bite cells in a peripheral smear is characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Oxidized, denatured hemoglobin forms Heinz bodies, which are 'bitten' out by splenic macrophages.

questions.questionText: A substitution of valine for glutamic acid at the 6th position of the beta-globin chain results in:
questions.explanation: This point mutation leads to polymerization of deoxygenated HbS.

questions.questionText: Reed-Sternberg cells are the pathognomonic finding in:
questions.explanation: These are large, binucleated 'owl-eye' cells characteristic of Classic Hodgkin lymphoma.

questions.questionText: The Philadelphia chromosome [t(9;22)] forming the BCR-ABL fusion gene is the hallmark of:
questions.explanation: The BCR-ABL fusion protein has constitutive tyrosine kinase activity.

questions.questionText: Auer rods are cytoplasmic inclusions typically found in the blast cells of:
questions.explanation: Auer rods represent fused lysosomes/granules, highly specific for AML.

questions.questionText: Bence Jones proteinuria is classically associated with:
questions.explanation: Bence Jones proteins are free immunoglobulin light chains excreted in urine.

questions.questionText: The characteristic translocation in Burkitt lymphoma is:
questions.explanation: Translocation of c-myc directly behind the Ig heavy chain promoter drives proliferation.

questions.questionText: Which coagulation test is characteristically prolonged in Hemophilia A?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) affects the intrinsic pathway, tested by aPTT.

questions.questionText: The most common cause of right-sided heart failure is:
questions.explanation: Left heart failure increases pulmonary venous pressure, leading to right heart failure.

questions.questionText: Stable angina pectoris is typically caused by:
questions.optionA: Atherosclerotic plaque obstructing >70% of a coronary artery
questions.optionD: Plaque rupture with complete thrombus occlusion
questions.explanation: Fixed strictures cannot increase blood flow during exertion, causing reversible ischemia.

questions.questionText: The most specific and sensitive serum biomarker for acute myocardial infarction is:
questions.explanation: Troponins are highly sensitive and specific for myocardial damage.

questions.questionText: What is the most common complication and cause of death in the first few hours following an acute myocardial infarction?
questions.explanation: Electrical instability surrounding the ischemic area leads to fatal arrhythmias.

questions.questionText: Fibrinous pericarditis (a 'bread and butter' appearance) is a classic finding in:
questions.explanation: Fibrin-rich exudate causes a shaggy surface on the pericardium.

questions.questionText: Rheumatic fever is a consequence of pharyngeal infection by which organism?
questions.explanation: Antibodies against M-protein cross-react with cardiac tissue (molecular mimicry).

questions.questionText: The classic histological finding in acute rheumatic carditis is the:
questions.explanation: Aschoff bodies are granulomatous lesions containing Anitschkow cells (caterpillar cells).

questions.questionText: The most common cause of infective endocarditis in intravenous drug abusers is:
questions.optionA: Viridans streptococci involving the mitral valve
questions.optionB: Candida albicans involving the pulmonary valve
questions.optionC: Staphylococcus aureus involving the tricuspid valve
questions.explanation: S. aureus aggressively attacks the first valve it reaches from systemic venous entry.

questions.questionText: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is most often caused by mutations in genes encoding:
questions.explanation: HCM is an autosomal dominant disorder of sarcomere proteins.

questions.questionText: A 'tear' in the aortic intima with blood tracking into the media constitutes:
questions.explanation: This dissection creates a false lumen, often presenting with sudden 'tearing' chest pain.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is characteristic of Centriacinar emphysema?
questions.optionA: Strong association with smoking and involvement of the upper lobes
questions.optionB: Involvement of the lower lobes predominantly
questions.explanation: Smoke accumulates in the upper lobes, destroying the respiratory bronchioles.

questions.questionText: A 65-year-old male with a long smoking history presents with a chronic productive cough for 3 months in 2 consecutive years. The most likely diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: The clinical definition of chronic bronchitis requires this specific history.

questions.questionText: Curschmann spirals and Charcot-Leyden crystals in sputum are pathognomonic for:
questions.explanation: Curschmann spirals are mucus plugs; Charcot-Leyden crystals are from breakdown of eosinophils.

questions.questionText: Permanent abnormal dilation of bronchi and bronchioles caused by destruction of muscle and elastic tissue is termed:
questions.explanation: Usually secondary to severe or chronic necrotizing infections.

questions.questionText: Pneumoconiosis caused by inhalation of silica dust typically results in:
questions.optionB: Nodular fibrosis with 'eggshell' calcification in hilar lymph nodes
questions.explanation: Silicosis profoundly increases susceptibility to tuberculosis.

questions.questionText: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomas are the hallmark of:
questions.explanation: Sarcoidosis is a systemic disease of unknown etiology characterized by these findings.

questions.questionText: The most common type of primary lung cancer is:
questions.explanation: Adenocarcinoma is the most common lung cancer overall, particularly in women and non-smokers.

questions.questionText: Which lung cancer is centrally located, strongly associated with smoking, and may produce parathyroid hormone-related peptide (PTHrP)?
questions.explanation: Squamous cell carcinoma leads to hypercalcemia through PTHrP production.

questions.questionText: Small cell carcinoma of the lung typically presents with:
questions.optionB: A large peripheral mass with cavitation
questions.optionC: A favorable response to surgical resection
questions.optionD: Early early metastasis and paraneoplastic syndromes (e.g., ADH, ACTH)
questions.explanation: It is an aggressive neuroendocrine tumor rarely treated with surgery.

questions.questionText: The classic four stages of lobar pneumonia (pneumococcal) are sequentially:
questions.optionC: Congestion, Red Hepatization, Gray Hepatization, Resolution
questions.explanation: This defines the classic lobar pattern of Streptococcus pneumoniae.

questions.questionText: The most common site for a peptic ulcer is the:
questions.explanation: Duodenal ulcers are the most common type and are highly associated with H. pylori.

questions.questionText: A defining histological feature of Celiac disease (Gluten-sensitive enteropathy) is:
questions.explanation: Immune reaction to gliadin results in destruction of the absorptive villi.

questions.questionText: Crohn's disease classically involves:
questions.optionA: Mucosal inflammation beginning in the rectum
questions.optionD: Continuous involvement of the left colon
questions.explanation: Transmural inflammation leads to strictures, fistulas, and a 'cobblestone' mucosa.

questions.questionText: Ulcerative Colitis always involves the:
questions.explanation: UC starts in the rectum and spreads continuously proximally, limited to the mucosa/submucosa.

questions.questionText: A key complication of chronic Ulcerative Colitis is a high risk for developing:
questions.explanation: Long-standing UC has a significantly increased risk of dysplasia and colon cancer.

questions.questionText: Hirschsprung disease (Congenital aganglionic megacolon) is caused by a failure of:
questions.optionA: Neural crest cells to migrate into the distal bowel
questions.explanation: Absence of Meissner's and Auerbach's plexuses causes a functional obstruction.

questions.questionText: The most common precursor lesion to colorectal cancer is the:
questions.explanation: Most colorectal carcinomas arise from adenomas via the adenoma-carcinoma sequence.

questions.questionText: Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) is caused by a mutation in the:
questions.explanation: FAP patients develop hundreds of polyps; malignant transformation is 100%.

questions.questionText: A characteristic finding in Acute Appendicitis is:
questions.optionB: Neutrophilic infiltration of the muscularis propria
questions.explanation: Diagnosis requires transmural neutrophilic inflammation.

questions.questionText: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome is caused by a Gastrin-secreting tumor, most often located in the:
questions.explanation: Gastrinomas cause massive acid hypersecretion leading to refractory peptic ulcers.

questions.questionText: The classic presentation of Nephrotic Syndrome includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Hematuria is characteristic of Nephritic syndrome, not Nephrotic syndrome.

questions.questionText: Red blood cell casts in the urine are highly indicative of:
questions.explanation: RBC casts mean the RBCs were molded in the renal tubules, a sign of glomerular bleeding.

questions.questionText: The most common cause of Nephrotic syndrome in children is:
questions.explanation: MCD responds well to steroids and shows only effacement of podocyte foot processes on EM.

questions.questionText: A subepithelial 'lumpy-bumpy' deposit pattern on electron microscopy is typical for:
questions.explanation: Caused by subepithelial immune complex deposition 2-4 weeks after a strep infection.

questions.questionText: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules in the glomerulus are virtually pathognomonic for:
questions.explanation: These nodular glomerulosclerotic lesions are classic for diabetic kidney disease.

questions.questionText: Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN) is most commonly caused by:
questions.explanation: ATN is the most common cause of intrinsic acute kidney injury.

questions.questionText: The presence of 'muddy brown' granular casts in the urine is highly suggestive of:
questions.explanation: The sloughing of necrotic tubular epithelial cells forms these casts.

questions.questionText: White blood cell casts in the urine are a hallmark of:
questions.explanation: WBC casts indicate inflammation in the kidney itself, differentiating pyelonephritis from lower UTI.

questions.questionText: The most common type of renal stone is composed of:
questions.explanation: Calcium stones (mostly oxalate) account for ~80% of kidney stones.

questions.questionText: Clear cell Renal Cell Carcinoma is often associated with the loss of which tumor suppressor gene?
questions.explanation: VHL mutation leads to increased HIF-1 expression and highly vascular tumors.

questions.questionText: Which hepatitis virus is a DNA virus?
questions.explanation: HBV is a partially double-stranded DNA hepadnavirus. The others are RNA viruses.

questions.questionText: The hallmark histological sign of severe acute viral hepatitis is:
questions.explanation: Councilman bodies are intensely eosinophilic, apoptotic hepatocytes.

questions.questionText: Liver cirrhosis is characterized by:
questions.optionC: Bridging fibrous septa and regenerative hepatocyte nodules
questions.explanation: Fibrosis and disrupted architecture defined by regenerative nodules equal cirrhosis.

questions.questionText: Mallory-Denk bodies (Mallory hyaline) are characteristic, though not pathognomonic, of:
questions.explanation: These eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions consist of tangled intermediate cytokeratin filaments.

questions.questionText: A key mechanism of liver injury in hemochromatosis is:
questions.explanation: Excess iron generates reactive oxygen species leading to lipid peroxidation and fibrosis.

questions.questionText: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC) is strongly associated with which autoantibody?
questions.explanation: AMA is present in >90% of patients with PBC, attacking intrahepatic bile ducts.

questions.questionText: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) is strongly associated with which other disease?
questions.explanation: There is a strong clinical link between PSC and Ulcerative Colitis.

questions.questionText: Which primary liver tumor is most common in patients with underlying cirrhosis and chronic HBV/HCV?
questions.explanation: HCC primarily arises in the setting of chronic liver disease/cirrhosis.

questions.questionText: A 35-year-old female on oral contraceptives presents with an incidentally found solitary liver mass. It may rupture and bleed. The likely diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Hepatic adenomas are strongly associated with OCP use and carry a risk of rupture.

questions.questionText: Gallstones composed primarily of cholesterol are most strongly associated with:
questions.optionD: Obesity, female gender, and multiparity ('Female, Fat, Forty, Fertile')
questions.explanation: These are classic risk factors for cholesterol stone formation.

questions.questionText: The most common cause of hyperthyroidism (thyrotoxicosis) is:
questions.explanation: Graves' disease is an autoimmune condition caused by stimulating TSI antibodies.

questions.questionText: Hashimoto thyroiditis is histologically characterized by:
questions.optionB: Extensive lymphocytic infiltrate with germinal centers and Hurthle cells
questions.optionC: Papillary projections and Orphan Annie eye nuclei
questions.explanation: The destruction of the thyroid gland is marked by prominent lymphoid aggregates.

questions.questionText: The most common type of thyroid carcinoma is:
questions.explanation: Papillary carcinoma accounts for about 80% of all thyroid cancers and has an excellent prognosis.

questions.questionText: Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from which cell type?
questions.explanation: Medullary carcinomas secrete calcitonin and may present as part of MEN 2 syndromes.

questions.questionText: Primary hyperparathyroidism is most often caused by a:
questions.explanation: A solitary benign parathyroid adenoma is responsible for >80% of cases.

questions.questionText: Cushing syndrome caused specifically by an ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma is called:
questions.explanation: Cushing *disease* specifically refers to a pituitary origin for the hypercortisolism.

questions.questionText: Primary chronic adrenocortical insufficiency is known as:
questions.explanation: Addison disease involves autoimmune destruction of the entire adrenal cortex.

questions.questionText: Pheochromocytomas are tumors of the adrenal medulla that secrete:
questions.explanation: They cause episodic hypertension, palpitations, and sweating.

questions.questionText: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus is primarily caused by:
questions.optionB: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells
questions.explanation: Absolute insulin deficiency occurs due to T-cell mediated destruction of beta cells.

questions.questionText: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 1 (MEN 1 - Wermer syndrome) involves tumors of the '3 Ps':
questions.explanation: These are the classic sites for tumors in the MEN 1 syndrome.
